name: Fatty acid β-oxidation (mitochondrial)
summary: Mitochondrial spiral that activates a fatty acid, imports it via the carnitine shuttle, and iteratively removes two-carbon units as acetyl-CoA, generating FADH2 and NADH.
description: Mitochondrial β-oxidation is the principal route for oxidizing long-chain fatty acids to fuel ATP synthesis, especially in fasting, exercise, and in heart, liver, and skeletal muscle. A fatty acid is first activated in the cytosol to a fatty acyl-CoA at the cost of two high-energy phosphate bonds (ATP → AMP + PPi). Because acyl-CoA cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane, it is handed to carnitine by carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1 (CPT1) on the outer membrane — the committed, rate-limiting, malonyl-CoA-regulated step — shuttled across by the carnitine–acylcarnitine translocase, and reconverted to acyl-CoA by CPT2 in the matrix. Each turn of the β-oxidation spiral then runs four reactions — FAD-linked oxidation (acyl-CoA dehydrogenase), hydration (enoyl-CoA hydratase), NAD+-linked oxidation (3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase), and thiolytic cleavage (3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase) — shortening the chain by two carbons and releasing one acetyl-CoA, one FADH2, and one NADH. For palmitoyl-CoA (C16) shown here, the cycle repeats seven times to yield 8 acetyl-CoA, 7 FADH2, and 7 NADH. Long-chain steps are carried out by very-long-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (VLCAD) and the membrane-bound trifunctional protein (HADHA/HADHB); medium- and short-chain intermediates are handled by soluble matrix isozymes (MCAD/SCAD, ECHS1, HADH, ACAA2). The FADH2 electrons enter the respiratory chain via electron-transfer flavoprotein (ETF) and ETF-ubiquinone oxidoreductase; acetyl-CoA is oxidized in the TCA cycle or, in fasting liver, converted to ketone bodies.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 17 (Fatty Acid Catabolism)

references.title: A general introduction to the biochemistry of mitochondrial fatty acid β-oxidation
The mitochondrial carnitine palmitoyltransferase system. From concept to molecular analysis

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Fatty acid activation to acyl-CoA occurs on the cytosolic face of the outer mitochondrial membrane.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial outer membrane
compartments.description: Site of acyl-CoA synthetase (ACSL1) and CPT1.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial intermembrane space
compartments.description: Acylcarnitine formed by CPT1 is presented here for translocation.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial inner membrane
compartments.description: Site of the carnitine–acylcarnitine translocase, CPT2, and the membrane-bound oxidation machinery.

compartments.name: Nucleus
compartments.description: Site of PPARα-driven transcription of β-oxidation genes.

enzymes.name: Carnitine O-palmitoyltransferase 1, liver isoform (CPT1A)
enzymes.description: Committed, rate-limiting step of mitochondrial fatty-acid oxidation; allosterically inhibited by malonyl-CoA.

enzymes.name: Carnitine–acylcarnitine translocase (CACT / SLC25A20)
enzymes.description: Antiporter exchanging matrix carnitine for intermembrane-space acylcarnitine.

enzymes.name: Very-long-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (VLCAD)
enzymes.description: Membrane-bound flavoprotein catalyzing the first oxidation of long-chain (C14–C20) acyl-CoAs.

enzymes.name: Trifunctional enzyme subunit alpha (MTPα; LCEH + LCHAD)
enzymes.family: mitochondrial trifunctional protein / enoyl-CoA hydratase + 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase
enzymes.description: Alpha subunit of the trifunctional protein: long-chain enoyl-CoA hydratase and long-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase activities (steps 2 and 3 for long-chain substrates).

enzymes.name: Trifunctional enzyme subunit beta (MTPβ; LCKAT)
enzymes.description: Beta subunit of the trifunctional protein: long-chain 3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase activity (step 4 for long-chain substrates).

enzymes.name: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (MCAD)
enzymes.description: Soluble matrix flavoprotein; first oxidation of medium-chain (C6–C12) acyl-CoAs. Deficiency (MCADD) is the most common inherited fatty-acid oxidation disorder.

enzymes.name: Enoyl-CoA hydratase, short chain (crotonase, ECHS1)
enzymes.description: Soluble short-chain enoyl-CoA hydratase (step 2 for shorter-chain intermediates).

enzymes.name: 3-Hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase, short chain (HADH)
enzymes.description: Soluble medium/short-chain 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase (step 3 for shorter-chain intermediates).

enzymes.name: 3-Ketoacyl-CoA thiolase, mitochondrial (ACAA2)
enzymes.description: Soluble medium/short-chain 3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase (step 4 for shorter-chain intermediates).

enzymes.name: Electron transfer flavoprotein subunit alpha (ETFA)
enzymes.description: FAD-bearing subunit of ETF; accepts electrons from acyl-CoA dehydrogenases.

enzymes.name: Electron transfer flavoprotein-ubiquinone oxidoreductase (ETF-QO)
enzymes.description: Passes electrons from reduced ETF to ubiquinone, linking β-oxidation to the respiratory chain.

enzymes.name: Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPARα)
enzymes.description: Fatty-acid-activated nuclear receptor / transcription factor that induces the fatty-acid oxidation gene program during fasting.

enzymes.name: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase 2 (ACC2 / ACACB)
enzymes.description: Mitochondria-associated isoform that produces the malonyl-CoA pool inhibiting CPT1.

enzymes.name: AMP-activated protein kinase, catalytic subunit α2 (AMPK / PRKAA2)
enzymes.description: Cellular energy sensor; phosphorylates and inhibits ACC, lowering malonyl-CoA and activating fatty-acid oxidation.

genes.name: hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase trifunctional multienzyme complex subunit alpha

complexes.name: Mitochondrial trifunctional protein (MTP/TFP)
complexes.description: α4β4 hetero-octamer bound to the inner membrane; carries out the last three steps of long-chain β-oxidation.

complexes.name: Electron transfer flavoprotein (ETF)
complexes.description: αβ heterodimer that shuttles electrons from acyl-CoA dehydrogenases to ETF-ubiquinone oxidoreductase.

reactions.name: Fatty acid activation (long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase)
reactions.equation: Palmitate + CoA + ATP -> Palmitoyl-CoA + AMP + PPi + H+

cofactors.role: adenylylation; cleaved to AMP + PPi (two high-energy bonds)

provenance.notes: Made effectively irreversible in vivo by subsequent hydrolysis of PPi to 2 Pi by inorganic pyrophosphatase.

reactions.name: Carnitine palmitoyltransferase I (CPT1) — committed, rate-limiting step

provenance.notes: Kinetically controlled (not thermodynamically irreversible); flux is governed by allosteric inhibition by malonyl-CoA.
Regenerates matrix acyl-CoA and frees carnitine for return by the translocase.

cofactors.role: electron acceptor; FADH2 electrons pass to ETF

provenance.notes: Introduces a trans-Δ2 double bond. Chain-length isozymes: VLCAD (C14–C20), MCAD (C6–C12), SCAD (C4–C6).
Stereospecific hydration to the L-(S)-3-hydroxy isomer. Long-chain activity by the trifunctional protein (HADHA); short-chain by ECHS1.
Long-chain activity (LCHAD, EC 1.1.1.211) by the trifunctional protein (HADHA); medium/short-chain (EC 1.1.1.35) by HADH.

Strongly exergonic thiolysis that drives the spiral forward; releases acetyl-CoA and a fatty acyl-CoA two carbons shorter (myristoyl-CoA), which re-enters at step 1. For palmitoyl-CoA, 7 cycles yield 8 acetyl-CoA, 7 FADH2, 7 NADH. Long-chain activity by the trifunctional protein (HADHB); medium/short-chain by ACAA2.

regulations.mechanism: Malonyl-CoA — the product of acetyl-CoA carboxylase and the committed intermediate of fatty-acid synthesis — allosterically inhibits CPT1, blocking acyl-CoA entry into the mitochondrion. This reciprocally coordinates β-oxidation with lipogenesis: when malonyl-CoA is high (fed state), fat is synthesized rather than burned.

A high mitochondrial NADH/NAD+ ratio (e.g., when the respiratory chain is saturated) causes product inhibition of the 3-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase step, slowing β-oxidation to match electron-transport-chain capacity.
A high acetyl-CoA/CoA ratio inhibits 3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase (product inhibition), coupling β-oxidation flux to downstream demand for acetyl-CoA in the TCA cycle and ketogenesis.

AMPK, activated by a rising AMP/ATP ratio (low energy charge), phosphorylates and inhibits acetyl-CoA carboxylase 2. This lowers malonyl-CoA, relieving CPT1 inhibition and switching on fatty-acid oxidation when the cell needs ATP.
Long-chain fatty acids and their CoA/acyl derivatives are endogenous agonist ligands of PPARα, so abundant fatty acid feeds forward to induce the transcriptional program for its own oxidation.
During fasting, activated PPARα induces transcription of CPT1A, raising the capacity for mitochondrial fatty-acid import.
MCAD (ACADM) is a classic PPARα target gene; its induction coordinately raises β-oxidation enzyme levels in the fasted, fatty-acid-rich state.

relations.note: Alpha subunit of the mitochondrial trifunctional protein.
Beta subunit of the mitochondrial trifunctional protein.
FAD-bearing alpha subunit of ETF.
Beta subunit of ETF.
Chain-length-specific acyl-CoA dehydrogenases (very-long vs long chain).
Chain-length-specific acyl-CoA dehydrogenases (long vs medium chain).
Chain-length-specific acyl-CoA dehydrogenases (medium vs short chain).
Enoyl-CoA hydratase activity: soluble short-chain (ECHS1) vs long-chain trifunctional protein (HADHA).
3-Hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase activity: soluble short/medium-chain (HADH) vs long-chain trifunctional protein (HADHA).
3-Ketoacyl-CoA thiolase activity: soluble medium-chain (ACAA2) vs long-chain trifunctional protein (HADHB).
CPT1A gene encodes carnitine palmitoyltransferase 1A.
ACADM gene encodes MCAD.
HADHA gene encodes the trifunctional protein alpha subunit.
FADH2 electrons pass from acyl-CoA dehydrogenases to electron transfer flavoprotein.
ETF transfers electrons to ETF-ubiquinone oxidoreductase, then to the ubiquinone pool.
Each spiral turn releases acetyl-CoA, which is oxidized to CO2 in the TCA cycle.
In fasting liver, β-oxidation-derived acetyl-CoA is diverted to ketone-body synthesis.
FADH2 (via ETF/ETF-QO) and NADH feed electrons into the respiratory chain for ATP synthesis.
Malonyl-CoA, made by acetyl-CoA carboxylase in fatty-acid synthesis, is the key inhibitor of CPT1 — the node that reciprocally coordinates synthesis and oxidation.
Glucose–fatty-acid (Randle) cycle: acetyl-CoA and NADH from β-oxidation inhibit pyruvate dehydrogenase and (via citrate) PFK-1, sparing glucose when fatty acids are plentiful.
